Who I Am
A Student Who Believes in Growth

I am G.S.Adheesha — a Year 2 Higher Diploma in Information Technology student at SLIIT City University, Colombo. My journey in this Professional Skills module has been more than academic; it has been a mirror that reflects who I am, who I want to become, and the values I carry.

From learning how to craft a CV to understanding Emotional Intelligence, professional responsibilities, and the Code of Ethics that governs our profession, every lecture has added a new layer to my professional self.

This portfolio is my honest account of what I have learned, how I have applied it, and what each lesson means to me personally. It is not just a submission — it is a story.

01

Introduction to Professional Skills

Understanding what makes a professional — beyond technical knowledge

What I Learned

Professional skills — also called soft skills or employability skills — are the non-technical, interpersonal abilities essential for workplace success. They complement technical expertise.

  • Communication, teamwork, problem-solving, adaptability
  • Values guide our behaviour and decision-making
  • Beliefs shape how we perceive the world
  • Attitudes are our predispositions — positive or negative
  • Character is demonstrated through consistent actions
  • The Johari Window helps us understand self-awareness

Class Activity

We completed the Johari Window exercise — mapping what we know about ourselves versus what others see. It revealed blind spots I never noticed before.

How I Applied It

I began actively observing my own communication patterns in group projects. When working with CodeQ (Group 43) on MotoStock26, I noticed I tended to stay quiet during disagreements. Recognising this pushed me to speak up constructively.

My Reflection

"Before this lecture, I thought being good at coding was enough. I realised that technical skills get you to the interview, but professional skills get you the job — and keep it. The Johari Window was humbling. It made me want to actively seek feedback from my peers."

02

CV Writing

Crafting a document that opens doors

What I Learned

A CV is the first impression an employer gets — making it both an art and a strategy. Tailoring matters enormously.

  • Tailor every CV to the specific job role
  • Use keywords from the job description for ATS
  • Structure: Personal info → Education → Experience → Skills
  • Clear formatting, concise content, zero errors
  • Avoid irrelevant or outdated information

Class Activity

We created our own CVs and conducted a peer review session — giving and receiving constructive feedback.

How I Applied It

I wrote a formal CV targeted at an IT Teacher position at Good Shepherd Convent, highlighting my HDIT achievements and ability to teach both Sinhala and English mediums for Grades 6–11.

My Reflection

"Writing my CV for a teaching role taught me that a CV is not just a list — it is a story told in bullet points. I had to think carefully about what a school principal would value. A tailored CV is always stronger than a generic one."

03

Emotional Intelligence

Understanding and managing emotions — in yourself and others

What I Learned

Emotional Intelligence (EI) is the ability to perceive, understand and manage emotions. Goleman's five core competencies:

  • Self-Awareness — Recognising your emotions as they happen
  • Self-Regulation — Expressing emotions at the right time
  • Motivation — Being driven by intrinsic goals
  • Empathy — Sensing what others feel
  • Social Skills — Cooperating effectively with others

How I Applied It

During the Beach Cleanup Project, when a team member expressed frustration about task division, I used cognitive re-appraisal — reinterpreting their reaction as concern rather than conflict — allowing me to respond calmly.

My Reflection

"Aristotle said: being angry at the right person, at the right time, in the right way is not easy. EI taught me it means using emotions wisely. This lesson changed how I approach group conflict and how I lead myself."

04

Job Hunting & Interview Skills

Presenting your best self under pressure

What I Learned

93% of communication is non-verbal. The 6 C's of effective communication guide every interaction:

  • Clear, Concise, Correct, Complete, Courteous, Concrete
  • Active listening creates stronger impressions
  • Research the company and role beforehand
  • Use the STAR method: Situation, Task, Action, Result

Class Activity

Mock interviews — taking turns as interviewer and interviewee, scored using a structured rubric.

How I Applied It

"I have completed coursework in PHP and MySQL, where I developed a Spare Parts Inventory Module for a real client, Bimsara Motors." — Using specific examples rather than vague descriptions made my answers far stronger.

My Reflection

"The mock interview revealed that I spoke too fast when nervous and avoided eye contact. I practised in front of a mirror. Silence is not weakness — it shows you are thinking. This module genuinely changed how I communicate under pressure."

05

Grooming & Professional Etiquette

First impressions, personal branding, and the power of presence

What I Learned

  • First impressions form in seconds — appearance, posture, eye contact
  • Personal brand is what people think when your name is mentioned
  • Daily hygiene, nail care, oral care, and clothing condition all matter
  • Greeting etiquette: use titles, maintain appropriate eye contact
  • Digital etiquette: professionalism extends online
  • Respect diversity — different cultures, religions, and backgrounds

How I Applied It

Before our mid-review viva for MotoStock26, I planned my outfit the night before — neat, professional, and appropriate. I rehearsed my self-introduction and ensured my emails to lecturers were always formal.

My Reflection

"I now iron my clothes the night before any presentation. I used to think these things were shallow. But it is respect — for yourself, for the opportunity, and for the people in the room. That shift in thinking changed how I carry myself every day."

06

Dining Etiquette & Table Manners

Confidence at the table — formal and informal

What I Learned

  • BMW Rule — Bread (left), Meal (centre), Water (right)
  • Napkin goes on lap immediately upon sitting
  • Wait until all are served before eating
  • Phone away — always, no exceptions
  • American vs European utensil styles differ
  • Cultural differences — Japan, Middle East, Europe

How I Applied It

During a group lunch after our project presentation, I consciously applied what I learned — placed my napkin on my lap, waited for everyone to be served, and kept my phone face-down. Small changes, but noticeable ones.

My Reflection

"The BMW rule alone saved me from an embarrassing moment at a formal event. Etiquette is not about being perfect; it is about making others feel comfortable. Dining etiquette communicates attentiveness and cultural awareness."

07

Portfolio Management

Curating proof of who you are and what you can do

What I Learned

A portfolio is a curated collection of work, achievements, and reflections. Types include:

  • Personal Portfolio — creative work, talents, interests
  • Career/Dossier Portfolio — resume, cover letters, credentials
  • Employment Portfolio — work samples, evaluations
  • Assessment Portfolio — evidence of learning progress

Digital portfolios are more versatile. Platforms: LinkedIn, Weebly, Squarespace, Cargo.

How I Applied It

This very website is my application of this lesson. Instead of a static binder, I built a web-based portfolio that demonstrates both my IT skills and my professional development — every section deliberately structured and personally reflective.

My Reflection

"Creating this portfolio made me see my own journey more clearly. The portfolio format forced me to be honest — where did I actually grow, and where do I still need to? This reflection was as valuable as the content itself."

08

Mock Interviews & Self-Reflection

Practising until it feels natural

What I Learned

  • Interviewers evaluate both content and delivery simultaneously
  • Non-verbal cues: posture, eye contact, gestures, expression
  • Verbal cues: pace, clarity, use of examples, confidence
  • Structured answers (STAR method) are more persuasive
  • Self-reflection after practice reveals specific growth areas

How I Applied It

After each mock interview session, I wrote down three strengths and three weaknesses. I then deliberately practised weaknesses — speaking more slowly, making steady eye contact, and pausing before answering.

My Reflection

"The most uncomfortable moment was the silence after a question — I panicked and rushed my answer. Now I practise pausing deliberately. Silence is not weakness; it shows you are thinking."

09

Professional Responsibilities

By Nushkan Nismi — SLIIT, Faculty of Computing

What I Learned

A profession is a calling requiring specialised knowledge, academic preparation, and service to society. A professional possesses expert knowledge, works to standards, accepts ethical responsibilities, and serves society.

Professionalism refers to behaviour and attitude — commitment to quality work, ethical conduct, and accountability.

  • Computing professionals include software engineers, system analysts, DBAs, network engineers, and IT managers
  • Computing systems influence healthcare, transportation, financial systems, and infrastructure
  • Attributes of a profession: specialised knowledge, professional body, ethical standards, public trust, service orientation

Responsibilities of Computing Professionals

  • To Clients — deliver quality systems, protect data, maintain confidentiality
  • To Employers — perform competently, protect assets, follow policies
  • To Colleagues — respect, collaborate, avoid discrimination
  • To Society — protect public safety, avoid harmful systems, promote fairness

How I Applied It

Working on the MotoStock26 project for Bimsara Motors gave me a real taste of professional responsibility. I was accountable for delivering a working Spare Parts Inventory module that the client would rely on daily. Every bug I fixed was a commitment to quality; every test case I wrote was protecting the client from errors.

My Reflection

"Before this lecture, 'responsibility' felt abstract. Working on a real client project made it concrete. When I realised a bug in my code could disrupt Bimsara Motors' daily operations, I understood what it means to be professionally accountable. It is not just about delivering a project — it is about being trustworthy."

10

Code of Ethics

Professional ethical frameworks — CSSL, IEEE, ACM, BCS

What I Learned

Ethics is a set of beliefs about right and wrong behaviour — honesty, fairness, and respect. A Code of Ethics is a formal guideline for professional behaviour that defines acceptable conduct and helps resolve ethical dilemmas.

Professional codes aim to: inspire ethical behaviour, educate professionals, provide guidance, define responsibilities, and enable discipline.

Professional Bodies & Their Codes

  • CSSL — professionalism, honesty, competence, contribution to national development
  • IEEE — prioritise safety and welfare, be honest, reject bribery, avoid conflicts of interest
  • ACM — contribute to society, avoid harm, be honest, respect privacy and intellectual property
  • BCS — public interest, professional competence, duty to authority and profession

Ethical Decision Making

Ethical decisions require considering: Stakeholders, Consequences, Responsibilities, and Alternatives.

Common ethical dilemmas in computing include: releasing untested software, data privacy breaches, algorithm bias, and intellectual property misuse. Ethical awareness helps professionals prevent harm, maintain reputation, build trust, and improve decision quality.

My Reflection

"This lecture made me realise that as an IT professional, my work has real consequences for real people. The CSSL Code of Ethics is not a formality — it is a commitment. Learning that my responsibility extends beyond my employer to society changed how I think about every line of code I write and every system I build."

Community Engagement
Beach Cleanup Project
Project Name Beach Cleanup Initiative
Module Professional Skills · IT1215
Organised By Leo Club of SCU
Skills Applied Teamwork · EI · Communication · Leadership

As part of the Professional Skills module, our batch participated in a community Beach Cleanup project organised by the Leo Club of SLIIT City University. Armed with waste bags, rakes, and gloves, we worked together as a team to clean a stretch of beach — putting into practice the teamwork, emotional intelligence, communication skills, and sense of social responsibility we had been learning in class. This project connected the classroom to the real world in the most meaningful way.

🤝
Teamwork in Action
Coordinating with peers, dividing responsibilities, and supporting each other — exactly what Professional Skills teaches us.
🧠
Emotional Intelligence
Managing frustrations, staying motivated under challenging conditions, and empathising with team concerns in the field.
🌊
Social Responsibility
Demonstrating commitment beyond the classroom — connecting our professional skills to real community impact.

What I've Built
Projects with Real Stories
🏍️

MotoStock26 — Spare Parts Inventory System

A full-stack web application built for Bimsara Motors, a motorcycle spare parts and servicing shop in Sri Lanka. Developed as part of Group 43 CodeQ's HND project. My individual module was the Spare Parts Inventory — built with PHP and MySQL, featuring CRUD operations, category filtering, low stock alerts, and QA testing with 31 manual test cases. The project follows a Forest Luxe UI theme with a navy and gold design system.
